title: How to Use Intuition When Choosing a Home
meta_description: Stop ignoring the knot in your stomach when you tour a house. That tight chest, that urge to leave even when everything looks perfect is your intuition screaming at you. Learn how to trust it before you sign on the dotted line.
og_title: How to Use Intuition When Choosing a Home
og_description: Stop ignoring the knot in your stomach when you tour a house. That tight chest, that urge to leave even when everything looks perfect is your intuition screaming at you. Learn how to trust it before you sign on the dotted line.
body:
You Walk Into a House and Something Feels Off

You can't name it. The kitchen is renovated. The floors are new. The light is good. But your chest is tight, and you want to leave. You tell yourself you're being silly. You tell yourself to be rational. But the feeling won't budge.

That feeling? That's your intuition talking. And in my experience, it's usually right.

I'm Kalesh, and I teach people how to trust their gut when it matters most. If you're someone who feels things deeply - who walks into a room and instantly knows the energy is off, even when everything looks fine - then you already have the equipment you need. The problem isn't that you're too sensitive. The problem is that nobody taught you how to use that sensitivity as a tool.

Choosing a home is one of the biggest decisions you'll make. It's also one of the most intuitive. You're not just picking walls and a roof. You're picking the space where your nervous system will rest, where your dreams will unfold, where your life will happen. That's not a logical spreadsheet decision. That's a soul decision.

So let's talk about how to actually use your intuition when you're house hunting. Not in some vague, woo-woo way. In a real, practical, feet-on-the-ground way. Does that land?

Why Your Gut Already Knows More Than Your Brain

Here's the thing about intuition. It's not magic. It's pattern recognition that happens below the level of conscious thought. Your brain is taking in thousands of data points every second - the way light falls in a room, the sound of the street, the feel of the floorboards, the energy of the previous occupants. You can't process all of that consciously. But your body can.

So when you walk into a house and feel uneasy, it's not random. It's your system picking up on something your conscious mind missed. Maybe it's the layout that makes you feel trapped. Maybe it's the history of the house - a divorce, an illness, a death. Maybe it's the neighborhood energy that doesn't match yours. Your body knows.

The problem is that most of us have been trained to override that feeling. We've been told to be "rational." To make lists. To compare square footage and school districts and resale value. And sure, all of that matters. But if you ignore your gut, you can end up in a beautiful house that makes you feel miserable. I've seen it happen a hundred times.

Right?! It's like dating someone who looks perfect on paper but makes your soul shrink. You can't negotiate chemistry. You can't spreadsheet your way into feeling at home.

Your intuition isn't the enemy of smart decision-making. It's the part of you that knows what your spreadsheets can't measure.

Before You Step Foot in Any House

I tell my students to do something simple before they even start looking. Sit down for five minutes. Close your eyes. Take three deep breaths. Then ask yourself: What do I actually want to feel in my home?

Not what you want it to look like. What you want it to feel like.

Do you want to feel safe? Expansive? Creative? Grounded? Nurtured? Private? Connected to community?

Write it down. Get specific. "I want to feel like I can exhale when I walk in the door." "I want to feel like I can be myself without performance." "I want to feel like the space holds me, not the other way around."

This becomes your intuitive benchmark. When you walk into a house, you're not checking it against a list of features. You're checking it against how it makes you feel. And that's way more reliable than any real estate listing.

If you need help getting clear on what you're actually sensing, I recommend picking up a copy of The Highly Sensitive Person by Elaine Aron. It's a classic for a reason, and it'll help you understand why you feel things so strongly in the first place. See on Amazon (paid link)

The First Visit: Shut Up and Feel

When you walk into a house for the first time, here's what I want you to do. Nothing. Don't talk. Don't ask questions. Don't open cabinets. Don't check the closets. Just stand in the entryway and feel.

What does your body do? Does your chest relax or tighten? Do your shoulders drop or go up? Do you feel like you can breathe deeper, or does your breath get shallow?

These are your first impressions, and they're the most honest. Your conscious mind hasn't had time to start rationalizing yet. This is pure data from your nervous system.

Walk through the house slowly. Room by room. Pause in each space. Notice what shifts. Some rooms will feel good. Some won't. That's normal. But pay attention to the overall pattern. Does the house as a whole feel like it supports you, or does it feel like it's working against you?

I once had a student who walked into a beautiful Victorian house and immediately felt nauseous in the living room. She couldn't explain it. The room was bright, clean, beautifully decorated. But her body was screaming. She listened. She didn't buy the house. Later, she found out that the previous owner had died suddenly in that room. Her body knew before her brain did.

That's not spooky. That's sensitivity. And it's a gift if you know how to use it.

What to Do When Your Gut Says No (But Your Brain Says Yes)

This is where it gets tricky. Because sometimes your intuition says no, but the house checks every box. Good location. Good price. Good schools. Good bones. And you start telling yourself you're being irrational.

Here's what I want you to do instead. Don't ignore the no. Investigate it.

Ask yourself: What specifically is my body reacting to? Is it the layout? The energy of the street? The smell of the basement? The feeling of being watched? The way the light hits at a certain time of day?

Sometimes the no is a hard no - the house is wrong for you, period. But sometimes the no is a soft no - there's something specific that needs attention, and once you address it, the yes can come through.

For example, I once worked with a woman who felt claustrophobic in every house she looked at. She thought something was wrong with her. But when we investigated, we realized she was picking up on the ceiling height. She needed tall ceilings to feel expansive. Once she knew that, she could look for houses with that feature. Her intuition wasn't broken. It was just giving her specific data she hadn't learned to decode yet.

If you're struggling to separate genuine intuition from fear or anxiety, I suggest You Are a Badass at Making Money by Jen Sincero. It's not about real estate specifically, but it's fantastic at helping you build trust in your own inner guidance. See on Amazon (paid link)

The Second Visit: Bring a Friend Who Feels

After you've done your first intuitive scan, bring someone else who's sensitive. Not a real estate agent (they're usually too invested in the sale). Not a spouse who's already made up their mind. Someone neutral who feels things.

Walk through the house together without talking. Then compare notes. Did you both feel the same heaviness in the back bedroom? Did they also get a weird feeling in the basement? Did they love the kitchen as much as you did?

This isn't about consensus. It's about confirmation. If two sensitive people feel the same thing, it's probably real. If you're the only one who feels something, it might be personal - something in your own history that's being triggered. Either way, it's useful information.

And here's a trick I love. Ask your friend to stand in the center of the house and just listen. Not with their ears. With their whole body. Ask them: What does this house want to say? You'd be surprised what comes through. Houses have personalities. They have memories. They have energy. And some houses are tired. Some are angry. Some are deeply peaceful. You want to know which one you're moving into.

The Night Visit: See How It Feels in the Dark

Here's something most people never do. Visit the house at night. Not during the day when everything looks sunny and hopeful. Go when it's dark. Go after 9 PM. Stand outside. Walk around the block. Feel the neighborhood energy when the sun goes down.

Does it feel safe? Does it feel alive? Does it feel dead? Does it feel like people are watching? Does it feel like you could walk to your car without your heart racing?

Then go inside if you can. Turn on the lights. Sit in the living room in the dark for five minutes. How does it feel? Different? Same? Better? Worse?

Houses change at night. The energy shifts. The sounds change. The light changes. And you need to know if this house can hold you when it's dark, when you're tired, when you're scared, when you're alone. Because that's when you'll really be living in it.

I can't tell you how many people bought a house they loved in the daylight and then felt creeped out at night. They ignored their intuition because they didn't want to be "silly." But that feeling of being creeped out is real. It's your system telling you something. Listen to it.

What If Your Intuition Says Yes and Your Brain Says No?

This is the other side of the coin. Sometimes you walk into a house that's a fixer-upper. The roof is old. The kitchen is terrible. The yard is overgrown. And your brain is screaming run. But your heart is singing.

What do you do?

You pay attention. Because that kind of yes is rare. That kind of yes is your soul saying this is where I want to grow. And a fixer-upper isn't just a house. It's a relationship. It's a commitment to transformation. If you feel that yes, it might be worth the work.

But you need to be honest with yourself. Are you saying yes to the house itself, or are you saying yes to the fantasy of fixing it up? Are you romanticizing the potential? Or do you genuinely feel at home in its bones?

Spend time in the house as it is, not as it could be. Sit on the floor. Touch the walls. Feel the structure. If the yes is still there, even in the mess, then you have your answer.

For more on how to trust that kind of inner knowing, I recommend The Gifts of Imperfection by Brené Brown. It's all about trusting yourself enough to live wholeheartedly, even when it doesn't make logical sense. See on Amazon (paid link)

The Energy of the Previous Occupants

This one makes people uncomfortable, but I'm going to say it anyway. Houses hold energy from the people who lived in them before. If a couple fought constantly in the kitchen, that kitchen might feel heavy. If someone died in the bedroom, that bedroom might feel sad. If a family was happy and loving, the house might feel warm and welcoming.

You don't have to be psychic to feel this. You just have to be sensitive. And if you're reading this, you probably are.

So when you walk through a house, pay attention to the emotional residue. Does the master bedroom feel peaceful or tense? Does the living room feel joyful or hollow? Does the basement feel creepy or just unfinished?

If you feel something heavy, you can clear it. Sage, sound, intention, time - all of that works. But you need to know what you're walking into. Don't buy a house that's soaked in misery unless you're ready to do the clearing work. And be honest with yourself about whether you have the energy for that.

The Land Itself Matters

The house is important. But the land it sits on is even more important. The land has its own history, its own energy, its own memory. And the land will still be there long after the house is gone.

When you visit a property, walk the land. Feel the ground under your feet. Is it flat or sloped? Wet or dry? Does it feel alive or dead? Does it feel like it wants to be built on, or does it feel like it wants to be left alone?

I've known people who bought houses on land that felt wrong, and they never felt settled. No matter how much they renovated the house, the land itself was working against them. That's not something you can fix with paint and new flooring.

So trust what you feel when you're standing on the dirt. Your feet know things your eyes can't see.

The Final Test: Can You Exhale Here?

Here's my simplest test for whether a house is right for you. Stand in the center of the living room. Take a deep breath in. Then let it out slowly. Don't force anything. Just breathe.

Does your exhale feel easy? Does your breath feel like it can go all the way out? Or does it feel like something is blocking it, like there's a knot in your chest that won't release?

If you can exhale fully, that's a good sign. That means your nervous system feels safe enough to relax. If you can't exhale, that's a red flag. Your body is telling you this space isn't safe for your nervous system.

You can override that feeling with logic. You can tell yourself it's a good investment. You can tell yourself you'll get used to it. But your body will never get used to it. Your body will hold that tension for as long as you live there. And that tension will cost you more than any mortgage payment.

Trust the Feeling That Never Goes Away

Here's the thing I want you to take away from all of this. Your intuition isn't a luxury. It's not something you consult when you have time. It's the most practical tool you have for making a decision this big. Because a house isn't just an asset. It's the container for your life. And if the container doesn't fit, nothing inside it will feel right.

So when you're house hunting, don't just look with your eyes. Feel with your body. Listen with your whole self. And trust the feeling that never goes away, even when you try to talk yourself out of it.

That feeling is your compass. And it will never lead you wrong.

If you want to go deeper into trusting your intuition for life's big decisions, I've put together a free assessment to help you figure out where you're blocking your own inner knowing. Take the intuition assessment here and see where your sensitivity is trying to guide you next.
